title: The long battle between Nigel Farage and Kemi Badenoch
gist: Andrew Adonis argues the year-long narrative of Nigel Farage's Reform UK destroying the Conservatives has reversed: Kemi Badenoch has recovered by co-opting Farage's platform while positioning as 'responsible,' Reform has underperformed in recent elections, and the two parties are now in an uneasy stalemate rooted in a Brexit-era electoral rupture that neither can fully overcome.

insights.claim: The current standoff between Reform UK and the Conservatives traces back to Farage's Ukip capturing a large slice of the Tory electoral base — particularly poorer 'left behind' provincial England — years before the 2016 Brexit referendum, meaning the referendum turned a pre-existing split into a lasting rupture rather than creating it.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Frames the Farage-Tory rivalry as structural and decade-long rather than a recent phenomenon, implying it won't be resolved by short-term tactics from either party.
insights.locator: As ever with politics these days, it goes back to Brexit

insights.claim: Boris Johnson's 2019 election landslide, which appeared to restore the old Tory electoral coalition, was only possible because Farage's Brexit Party unilaterally stood down candidates in Tory-held seats to get Brexit 'over the line' — not because the underlying voter split had actually healed.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Explains why the Tory-Reform split reasserted itself so quickly after 2019 — the 2019 coalition was a tactical truce, not a durable realignment.
insights.locator: Boris Johnson appeared briefly to have restored the old Tory electoral coalition

insights.claim: By the 2024 election the Conservatives were being squeezed simultaneously from the right by Farage's Reform UK and from the centre by Labour and the Liberal Democrats, who seized historic Tory territory in London and the affluent Home Counties — a two-front collapse that went beyond merely reversing the 2019 gains.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Illustrates that the Tory vote coalition fractured along both a populist-right axis and an affluent-centrist axis simultaneously, which is why by end-2024 some analysts speculated the party could be squeezed out of existence.
insights.locator: On the Tory side, 2024 saw significant losses not only to Farage

insights.claim: Over the past year Kemi Badenoch has recovered from a poor first year as Tory leader by adopting much of Farage's platform (including pledging to expel intra-party 'wets' who disagree) while successfully positioning herself as more 'responsible' than Farage — narrowing the space he can occupy.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Signals a viable Tory counter-strategy to Reform: co-opt the substance of Reform's platform while claiming the competence/responsibility high ground, rather than trying to out-populist Farage.
insights.locator: However, over recent months all this has changed

insights.claim: Farage has become mired in sleaze controversies (notably scrutiny over a £5m donation) and has lurched further right on mass immigrant deportations, and the wave of Tory MP and councillor defections to Reform that once looked unstoppable has slowed rather than turned into a flood.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: A concrete leading indicator (defection rate) that Reform's momentum against the Tories has stalled, useful for tracking the durability of the populist-right threat to the Conservatives.
insights.locator: The series of Tory MP and local councillors defectors slowed

insights.claim: In May's local elections Reform did not achieve the surge among the young and in cities that some expected; instead Zack Polanski's Greens emerged as the populist party of choice for younger, urban voters, while the Lib Dems held their 2024 gains in affluent former-Tory areas without advancing further.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Suggests Reform's populist appeal is capped at its provincial, older, less affluent base and is not converting into a broader youth/urban coalition — a ceiling on the party's growth.
insights.locator: Reform cleaned up in its provincial heartlands

insights.claim: In the Makerfield byelection, the further-right challenger party Restore Britain ate into Reform's expected vote share — one in a long line of recent byelections that Reform has underperformed in or lost outright.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Reform now faces flanking pressure from an even further-right challenger, complicating its ability to consolidate the populist-right vote unopposed.
insights.locator: in the recent Makerfield byelection, Restore Britain

insights.claim: Adonis argues there is now a durable 'Tory bedrock' of support in London and southern England that is structurally beyond Farage's reach, because his populism and personal style are too off-putting to voters in these more affluent regions — meaning Reform's ceiling on the national map is geographically bounded regardless of its provincial strength.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Pushes back on narratives of Farage as an unstoppable national force; investors/analysts modeling UK political risk should weight in a regional ceiling on Reform's electoral potential.
insights.locator: There now appears to be a Tory bedrock

insights.claim: Adonis characterizes the Farage-Badenoch relationship as a stand-off of 'partial occupations' — each aspires to conquer the other's territory but neither has proven strong enough to do so, with Farage's defensive Clacton byelection fight (against Count Binface) reflecting his currently weakened state.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Frames the near-term UK right-of-centre political landscape as a contested, unresolved two-party fight rather than a Reform breakout — worth monitoring the Clacton byelection outcome as a signal of Farage's trajectory.
insights.quote: Each aspires to conquer the other, but has so far proved strong enough to achieve only partial occupations.
insights.locator: The net effect is an uneasy stand-off
